name: GitLab
description: DevSecOps platform combining Git hosting, merge request review and CI/CD; yields audit events, protected branch and approval configuration, and pipeline security evidence.

steps.action: In the top bar, select Search or go to and find your group, then in the left sidebar select Secure > Audit events (Premium or Ultimate tier)
steps.security_note: Group audit events require the Owner role; consider a dedicated audit account rather than sharing a personal Owner login
steps.audit_note: GitLab retains audit events indefinitely, but a regular export cadence keeps evidence packages current

steps.action: Filter events by member, project and date range, then select Export as CSV to download the audit events
steps.audit_note: CSV exports use UTC timestamps; record the export date and filter criteria alongside the file

steps.action: For branch protection evidence, navigate to the project > Settings > Repository, expand Branch rules and screenshot the protected branch configuration
steps.audit_note: Changes to protected branches also appear in the audit event log, so the screenshot and export corroborate each other

steps.action: For merge request approval evidence, navigate to the project > Settings > Merge requests, expand Merge request approvals and screenshot the approval rules (required approvers, eligible groups)
On GitLab Self-Managed, additionally capture instance-level audit events: select Admin, then in the left sidebar select Monitoring > Audit events, and export to CSV
Upload the CSV exports and screenshots to the evidence repository with the evidence ID reference
steps.security_note: Check screenshots for visible personal access tokens, CI/CD variable values or clone URLs containing embedded credentials before uploading

L2.title: GitLab Scheduled Evidence Exports via Pipeline Schedules

steps.action: Note that GitLab has no natively scheduled audit event report; the closest built-in mechanism is a pipeline schedule. In an evidence project, navigate to Build > Pipeline schedules and create a monthly schedule (cron expression)
steps.permissions_required: Developer (create schedule); Maintainer to manage CI/CD settings

steps.action: Add a minimal CI job to the scheduled pipeline that downloads the group audit events via the API and saves the output as a job artifact with a defined expiry (expire_in)
steps.security_note: Store the access token as a masked and protected CI/CD variable (project Settings > CI/CD > Variables), never in the .gitlab-ci.yml file
steps.audit_note: Job artifacts expire; copy each export into the evidence repository so retention is not dependent on artifact settings

steps.action: For compliance posture evidence, navigate to the group > Secure > Compliance center and export the compliance status and violations reports on the same monthly cadence (Ultimate tier)
Enable pipeline failure notifications (email or integration) for the evidence project so a missed scheduled export is noticed immediately
steps.audit_note: A gap in monthly exports weakens the evidence trail; treat schedule failures as an incident to investigate

steps.action: File each month's export in the evidence repository and record the schedule run ID so the collection is traceable to the pipeline that produced it
Create a group access token: navigate to the group > Settings > Access tokens, grant the Owner role with the read_api scope and set an expiry date (Premium or Ultimate tier)
steps.security_note: Store the token in a secrets manager and rotate before expiry; group access tokens act as bot users and avoid tying automation to a personal account

steps.action: Schedule a daily script that calls GET https://gitlab.com/api/v4/groups/:id/audit_events with created_after and created_before parameters, passing the token in the PRIVATE-TOKEN header and using keyset pagination
steps.permissions_required: Group access token with Owner role and read_api scope
steps.audit_note: Date filtering is limited to a 30-day rolling window per query, so a daily pull with overlapping windows avoids missed events

steps.action: Collect configuration evidence alongside events: GET /projects/:id/protected_branches for branch protection state on each in-scope project
steps.permissions_required: read_api scope; Maintainer-level access to the projects

steps.action: Export merge request approval configuration via GET /projects/:id/approval_rules to evidence enforced peer review requirements
Capture CI/CD pipeline security evidence via GET /projects/:id/pipelines (status, source, SHA) to demonstrate that security scan stages run on protected branches

Add error handling and alerting: alert on non-200 responses, authentication failures and unexpectedly empty result windows, and validate date continuity between consecutive runs before storing the output with a checksum
steps.audit_note: Recording checksums and run metadata makes each collected file independently verifiable

L4.title: GitLab Continuous Audit Event Streaming Pipeline

steps.action: Configure audit event streaming: navigate to the top-level group > Secure > Audit events, select the Streams tab and add a destination — HTTP endpoint (with custom headers), Google Cloud Logging or AWS S3 (Ultimate tier)
steps.security_note: Use dedicated service credentials for the destination and restrict who can modify stream configuration

steps.action: On the receiving endpoint, validate the X-Gitlab-Event-Streaming-Token header against the destination's verification token before accepting events
steps.security_note: Rejecting requests without the correct verification token prevents forged audit events entering the evidence store

steps.action: On GitLab Self-Managed or Dedicated, additionally configure instance-level audit event streaming from the Admin area so administrator actions are captured continuously
Run a daily reconciliation job comparing streamed event counts against the audit events API for the same window, alerting on any gap
steps.audit_note: Streaming destinations do not guarantee delivery; reconciling against the API is what demonstrates completeness to an auditor

steps.action: Build a compliance dashboard from the API collectors: protected branch coverage, merge request approval rule compliance and pipeline security scan coverage across all in-scope projects, with alerts on drift
steps.audit_note: Trend data over time demonstrates sustained control operation rather than point-in-time compliance

steps.action: Quarterly, review stream destinations and custom headers, rotate group access tokens and destination credentials, and review the top-level group Owner list
steps.security_note: Streaming destinations receive the full audit trail; treat destination credentials with the same care as administrator accounts
